questions.stem: What are the four main components of a computer system?
options: Keyboard, mouse, monitor, printer
CPU, memory (RAM), storage, and input/output devices
Transistors, capacitors, resistors, and diodes
Python, Java, C++, and JavaScript
questions.explanation: A computer system has four core components: the CPU (processes instructions), memory/RAM (stores data currently being used), storage (hard drive/SSD, stores data permanently), and I/O devices (keyboard, screen, etc.). Understanding these components helps you reason about where performance bottlenecks occur.

questions.stem: What is the key difference between RAM and storage (hard drive/SSD)?
options: RAM stores data permanently; storage is temporary
RAM is much faster but volatile (loses data when power is off); storage is slower but persistent
RAM is used for programs; storage is used for the operating system
They are the same thing with different names
questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is extremely fast (nanosecond access) but loses its contents when powered off — it's for data currently in use. Storage (HDD/SSD) is slower (microseconds to milliseconds) but persistent. When you open a program, it's copied from storage into RAM so the CPU can access it quickly.

questions.stem: Why are GPUs preferred over CPUs for training neural networks?
options: GPUs have higher clock speeds than CPUs
GPUs have thousands of simple cores optimised for parallel operations, while CPUs have a few powerful cores for sequential logic
GPUs have more RAM than CPUs
CPUs cannot perform floating-point arithmetic
questions.explanation: CPUs have 8–64 powerful cores optimised for sequential tasks with complex branching. GPUs have thousands of simpler cores designed to run the same operation on many data points simultaneously — perfect for matrix multiplications in neural networks. Training a model involves billions of parallel multiply-add operations, which GPUs handle 100–1000× faster than CPUs.

questions.stem: What is the Von Neumann architecture, and what bottleneck does it create?
options: It separates CPU and GPU — the bottleneck is moving data between them
It stores both program instructions and data in the same memory — the bottleneck is the bus between CPU and memory
It requires all programs to be written in assembly language
It limits computers to processing one instruction at a time forever
questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture uses a single shared memory for both instructions and data. The CPU must constantly fetch from memory, and the memory bus becomes a bottleneck — the 'Von Neumann bottleneck'. For AI workloads, this matters because LLM weights (gigabytes) must be streamed from VRAM to compute units continuously, making inference memory-bandwidth bound.

questions.stem: A program on a 64-bit CPU can address up to how much virtual memory?
options: 4 GB (same as 32-bit)
16 GB
16 exabytes (2^64 bytes — practically unlimited for current use)
1 TB exactly
questions.explanation: A 64-bit address space is 2^64 bytes = 16 exabytes (16 million terabytes). In practice, current hardware uses 48-bit physical addresses (256 TB), but this is still vastly larger than 32-bit's 4 GB limit. This is why 64-bit systems can train large models: you can map multi-gigabyte model weights into a single virtual address space.

questions.stem: What does 'memory bandwidth bound' mean for LLM inference?
options: The model is too large to fit on the GPU
The GPU's arithmetic units sit idle waiting for data to arrive from memory — the bottleneck is data transfer speed, not compute speed
The CPU is bottlenecking the GPU via the PCIe bus
The network connection is limiting inference throughput
questions.explanation: At batch size 1, LLM inference requires loading all model weights from GPU memory (VRAM) for every token generated. The arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per byte) is low (~1), far below the GPU's roofline. The compute units finish their calculations and then wait for the next layer's weights to arrive from HBM — this is memory bandwidth bound. Quantisation helps by reducing the bytes that need to be moved.
